comments.text:
This was a game I played against GM Daniel Naroditsky in Toronto. 

I had the White pieces.
The rook went to f8.

I went to a4, leaving my d-pawn! 

I'm going to win the kingside pawns instead!
Naroditsky developed the bishop...
Naroditsky gave a check...

He blockaded the pawn. 

This is a common defensive idea, blockading the passed pawn with a piece.
And I went after his kingside pawns.
And I played Nc5, attacking a6 and blocking the c-file.

Naroditsky played g6. 

The first idea is to play Bg7 to fianchetto. 

The second and most important idea is to play Bf5, attacking my queen. 

So, perceiving my opponent's intentions, I altered the position in my favor:

I took a pawn and now I'm up three pawns, winning the game. 

As you were able to gather from this game, the Trompowsky has some classic plans that come with the opening. 

In addition, during a middle game you can create small plans to improve your position slowly, as illustrated with the Rab1-Rb5 plan and a4-a5 to break the queenside apart.
If this trade happens...
Black recognized there's a pin, so he attacked my pinned piece...
Naroditsky captured.
And to this I responded with the Trompowsky!
I brought my king to e3.
Black went to c6...
I supported it with the c-pawn.
I pushed d5.
He kept pressure on f2.

And Naroditsky tried complicating matters, creating pressure on the diagonal. 

For example, if I bring my knight back, I can't castle kingside.
Naroditsky traded.
Naroditsky took the d-pawn.
Unfortunately for him, that's only a one move threat that I can easily deal with.
The king went to d7.
I got out of danger.
I placed my rook on a light square.
I captured on e6.
He took my rook...
My rook becomes active on the a-file and I'm pretty happy.
When he attacked the f3 pawn...
Naroditsky is defending laterally.
I recaptured. And now I have a massive center!
Black is shuffling.
I got my king up.
Naroditsky doubled rooks.
I got the f-pawn out of danger.
I improved mine.
I played g3, ensuring that no piece can land on the f4 square.
Black recaptures.
But like I said, I can effectively undermine the pawn chain.
He didn't take the bishop, but instead established a pawn in the center.
I threatened checkmate in one!
He developed the queenside knight.
It seems I can't defend...
He took on c3.
Not only does this propose a rook trade, but it allows my king up.
Black is stalling.
Black attacked the pinned c4 pawn.

And I pushed the pawn, undermining d5! 

If d5 is undermined, c4 will be weak. 

At this point there isn't much to be done. It seems g6 was too slow, and it didn't contemplate Bxf6 and e4 as my response.

So of course I didn't play that trade. 

That would be an example of a bad trade, and could even be featured in the trading pieces chapter of this course.
But like I said, counterattacking is key!
I began to push it.
But it's too late.
Yet I took another with check!
So I gave a check and defended my knight simultaneously.
He gave a check.
The king stepped out of the way, threatening Re8.
One of the ways of defending, as you'll see in the defending chapter, is counterattacking!
Naroditsky improved his king.
Black attacked my knight.
I began with 1. d4.
The king went to a safe square.
I accepted, because in doing so I worsen Black's structure.
Naroditsky took a pawn...
Since I won't make progress on h3, I moved my king back.
I stepped out of danger.

Black attacked my knight. 

His idea is to remove the defender.

And I decided to simplify. 

I'm up material. 

It'll be easier to win this way.
And he attacked b2.
I defended.
I defended.
I castled.
But after this check, the defense is quite clear.
I got out of the pin with a tempo.
My queen was attacked.
I developed my queenside knight.
He covered g4, preventing me from entering.
I gave a check, attacking e6 as well.
Naroditsky challenged my center.
Black proposed a trade and defended against the checkmate.
I took on f6!

And I supported my pawn, thinking of pushing it as well. 

In the endgame, some plans are well-known. In this case, I'm placing my rook behind my passed pawn.
I took on a6.
He stayed on the 3rd rank, as he should to prevent me from going Kf3.
And I win a pawn!
Naroditsky went 1...Nf6.
But I captured his.
He recaptured.
Maybe he wants to attack from the back...
He went Ne4, attacking the bishop...
I recaptured, attacking the queen...
I got my other knight out.
The king went to e7.
I defended c4.
I moved up, finally.

I continued with my plan of breaking the queenside apart. 

As you can see, sometimes you can play two or three plans simultaneously. 

Instead of seeing it as one big plan, you can see it as a set of smaller scale plans that fit well together. 

For example, a4-a5, Rab1-Rb5.
Black defended.
I moved my king to f1.
He stayed on the 3rd rank, with pressure on f3.
Hence I proposed a trade, with the same idea.
Now he's trying to control the 4th rank to prevent me from marching up the board!
I placed my rook opposite the e6 weakness.
a4 prepares a5 to break apart Black's queenside.
The knight returned to f6.
The e-pawn is now weak.
Naroditsky shuffled.
The king won't be able to defend its advance.

I got my king closer to my rook. 

Rd3 is the idea.

I recaptured on a5. 

And the plan to break the queenside apart has been a success.

Usually in the Trompowsky you get the London diamond pawn structure, with d4, e3 and c3. 

That's the benefit of knowing your opening: you have existing plans that are part of that opening.
The king went up the board.

Naroditsky attacked my queen! 

Instead, he wants me to play Qxb6, so he can open up his rook along the a-file!
And I defended my pawn...

I retreated with my knight. 

The first step before beginning a plan to overwhelm my opponent is defending my weaknesses. 

The knight on e4 defends c3, and now I can begin making plans of improving my rooks to attack Black.
I played a rook lift.
He gave a check.
Black recaptured.
Naroditsky pinned my knight.

The king went to d8. 

My c3 pawn is attacked by two pieces, queen and rook. 

What would you play here?
Black traded.
He attacked c3.
He attacked f3...
And I went h4, intending to recapture with the h-pawn and open up my rook!
Black went closer to his kingside pawns.
